ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for noodle-based dishes is the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for suan la fen.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of suan la fen is approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as consumed in Chinese noodle restaurants or street-food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Suan La Fen — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Suan La Fen (cr_id F05060125) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous glass noodle dish data and documented below.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; sweet potato starch noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for suan la fen. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on sweet potato starch glass noodles, which are the primary carbohydrate source. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021) report GI values of 40–50 for sweet potato starch noodles. Vinegar in the dressing is documented to further attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 10.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 16.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 338 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,183 mg sodium, representing roughly 51% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit suan la fen to a maximum of 200 g per occasion, request reduced soy sauce and pickled vegetables, and avoid adding extra condiments to keep total sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) from sweet potato glass noodles, 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, and vinegar-based dressing that attenuates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Suan la fen is suitable for people with type 2 diabetes when consumed in controlled portions of 200–250 g, avoiding added sugar or sweet sauces, as the low-GI glass noodles and vinegar dressing support a moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium content at 338 mg per 100 g poses a cardiovascular risk at full-bowl portions; fat content is modest at 2.18 g per 100 g with low saturated fat (~0.35 g), which is favourable.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume suan la fen in portions no larger than 200 g, request reduced-sodium preparation, and note that the low saturated fat and fibre content are positive factors that partially offset the sodium concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chili oil contains nightshade-derived capsaicin compounds; pickled vegetables contribute high sodium that may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request a chili-free, reduced-sodium version of suan la fen, as nightshade compounds and high sodium may aggravate joint inflammation and tissue swelling in RA-susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Suan La Fen (酸辣粉, suān là fěn) is a popular Chinese street-food noodle dish originating from Chongqing, China. The dish centres on thick, chewy sweet potato starch glass noodles served in a spicy-sour broth or dressing made from chili oil, black rice vinegar, soy sauce, sesame paste, garlic, and spring onion, topped with pickled mustard greens, peanuts, and sometimes crispy soybeans. The name literally translates as 'sour spicy noodles.' The texture is slippery and springy, with a bold flavour profile that is simultaneously hot, sour, savoury, and slightly nutty. Per 100 g, suan la fen provides 66 kcal, 1.4 g protein, 2.18 g fat, 10.3 g carbohydrate, 2.4 g dietary fibre, 0.7 g sugar, and 338 mg sodium. The sweet potato glass noodles have a low glycemic index (~45), making the dish relatively favourable for blood glucose management when portion-controlled. Dietary fibre at 2.4 g per 100 g supports gut health. Sodium is the primary clinical concern, with a standard 350 g bowl delivering approximately 1,183 mg sodium. The dish is vegetarian and dairy-free but contains peanuts, soy, sesame, and gluten from soy sauce. Clinically, it is suitable for type 2 diabetes with portion control, conditionally suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease with sodium reduction, and should be avoided or modified for rheumatoid arthritis due to nightshade ingredients.
common_user_queries: Is suan la fen suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in suan la fen?
Is suan la fen gluten free?
What are the noodles in suan la fen made from?
What is the GI of glass noodles?
Can I eat suan la fen if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id F05060125) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous sweet potato starch noodle data with clear documentation.
notes: Saturated fat (0.35 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central data for analogous sweet potato glass noodle dishes, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for a sufficiently close analogue. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under 'western' cuisine and ethnic tags; corrected to 'Chinese' with origin in Chongqing, China. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on sweet potato starch glass noodles per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for suan la fen was found in approved sources. GL per serving = (45 × 10.3 × 350) / 100 / 100 = 16.2 g.
